title: Introducing new hens to an existing flock
subtitle: Quarantine, gradual introduction, and managing the pecking order
excerpt: Mixing new birds with an established flock always triggers aggression. A three-week quarantine protects both groups from disease. A staged introduction over several days reduces serious injury and lets the flock renegotiate rank without a single traumatic confrontation.
sourceNotes: Gail Damerow, The Chicken Health Handbook, 2nd ed. (2015), chapter on flock management. APHA poultry biosecurity guidance (UK, 2023). Chris Graham, Keeping Chickens (2006), chapter on flock integration.

glossaryTerms.definition: The linear dominance hierarchy within a chicken flock. Every bird has a rank established through threat displays and physical pecking. A stable hierarchy reduces ongoing aggression; disrupting it by adding new birds restarts the ranking process.
Keeping new birds physically separate from the existing flock for a minimum of three weeks. This allows time for latent disease to become visible before the new birds mix with established birds.

Injurious pecking directed at another bird's feathers, especially the back and vent area. It can escalate from mild pulling to severe wounding. Overcrowding, boredom, and flock disruption are common triggers.

content.text: Chickens live by a strict
pecking order
. Every new bird is a threat to that order. Expect chasing, blocking from feeders, and some feather loss. Your job is to make the process slow enough that serious injury does not happen.
Step 1: Quarantine for three weeks
Put new birds in
quarantine

on arrival. Three weeks catches most respiratory diseases, Marek's disease signs, and external parasites before they reach your existing birds. The quarantine space must be out of sight, sound, and sharing-airspace distance of the main coop. Ten metres is a minimum; the other side of a solid wall is better.
During quarantine, watch for sneezing, nasal discharge, swollen faces, lice, or any bird that looks dull and stands apart. Handle new birds last and wash your hands between the two groups.
Step 2: See-but-no-touch phase

After three weeks with no health concerns, move the new birds into a temporary pen that shares a fence line with the main run. The birds can see and hear each other through wire but cannot make contact. Run this phase for at least five days, ideally ten. Aggression through a fence is noise; aggression in a shared space is injury.
Step 3: Supervised free-range contact

Open the dividing gate for the first time when both groups are slightly hungry. Do this in a large space, never in a confined run. Free range in a garden gives the new birds room to retreat. Watch for ten minutes. Sustained chasing that does not stop is a problem. Brief pecks and posturing that move on are normal.
Scatter feed across a wide area so subordinate birds can eat without being driven off. Put out two water points. This reduces resource competition during the most tense first days.
Step 4: Moving in at night

Move the new birds into the main coop after dark on the third or fourth day of supervised contact. Chickens are calm in the dark and will roost wherever they find space. By morning they wake as if they had always shared the space. This is the single most effective way to speed integration.

attrs.title: How long does the aggression last?
attrs.body: For most flocks, the serious posturing lasts three to seven days after first contact. A new stable hierarchy is usually in place within two weeks. If one bird is still being chased away from food after two weeks, remove her briefly, let the hierarchy restabilise, and reintroduce her as a fresh unknown bird. This sometimes resets the dynamic.

content.text: Managing feather pecking
Watch the back and vent areas of new birds closely in the first two weeks.
Feather pecking

that draws blood must be stopped immediately. Blood attracts more pecking. Remove the injured bird, treat the wound with an anti-peck spray, and keep her separate until fully healed. Returning her too soon restarts the cycle.
Reduce the risk by adding enrichment when new birds arrive. A block of hanging greens, a dust bath area, and scattered grain give all birds something to do other than peck each other.
Age and size differences

Mixing birds of very different ages is harder than mixing birds of similar size. Pullets are physically smaller and lower status than laying hens. Do not mix bantams with heavy breeds unless you can provide separate feeding and sleeping stations. The size difference makes serious injury much more likely.

entries.cue: One bird is cornered and cannot reach the feeder
entries.cause: She is at the bottom of the new hierarchy and the established birds block her
entries.fix: Add a second feeder at a different point in the run. Put it where the lowest-ranked bird tends to stand. This alone resolves most feeder-blocking problems within a few days.

entries.cue: New birds refuse to go into the coop at dusk
entries.cause: They are not yet confident about the new space; established birds may be blocking the pop-hole
entries.fix: Go out at dusk for the first week and shepherd the new birds in manually. After three or four nights they learn the routine and go in on their own.

entries.cue: Established hen is being harassed by new birds
entries.cause: New birds are larger, younger, or more numerous and are not the subordinates you expected
entries.fix: Run the see-but-no-touch phase for longer, up to two weeks. Give the original hen a separate space she can retreat to inside the run. Consider whether the size mismatch makes the integration unsuitable.
